title: Block (basketball)

infobox.value: or **blocked shot** occurs when a defensive player legally deflects a [field goal attempt](/wiki/Field_goal_(basketball) "Field goal (basketball)") from an offensive player to prevent a score. The defender is not allowed to make contact with the offensive player's hand (unless the defender is also in contact with the ball) or a *[foul](/wiki/Foul_(basketball) "Foul (basketball)")* is called. In order to be legal, the block must occur while the shot is traveling upward or at its apex. A deflected field goal that is made does not count as a blocked shot and simply counts as a successful field goal attempt for shooter plus the points awarded to the shooting team. For the shooter, a blocked shot is counted as a missed field goal attempt. Also, on a shooting foul, a blocked shot cannot be awarded or counted, even if the player who deflected the field goal attempt is different from the player who committed the foul. If the ball is heading downward when the defender hits it, it is ruled as [goaltending](/wiki/Goaltending "Goaltending") and counts as a made basket. Goaltending is also called if the block is made after the ball bounces on the backboard ([NFHS](/wiki/National_Federation_of_State_High_School_Associations "National Federation of State High School Associations") excepted; the NCAA also used this rule until the 2009–10 season).

Brittney Griner's 736 career blocks is recognized as the all-time **NCAA** record, women's or men's.[[10]](#cite_note-DONOVAN-10) Hall of Famer [Anne Donovan](/wiki/Anne_Donovan "Anne Donovan"), who played for [Old Dominion](/wiki/Old_Dominion_Lady_Monarchs_basketball "Old Dominion Lady Monarchs basketball") from 1979 to 1983, recorded 801 blocks while playing in the [AIAW](/wiki/Association_for_Intercollegiate_Athletics_for_Women "Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women"); therefore, her total is not recognized as an NCAA achievement.[[10]](#cite_note-DONOVAN-10)

["The Legacy of Dwyane Wade: Is He The Last Elite Shooting Guard?"](https://web.archive.org/web/20211116072936/https://dish2swish.com/the-legacy-of-dwyane-wade/). *dish2swish.com*. 10 July 2016. Archived fr

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defensive action preventing a score

For the illegal defensive contact, see [Personal foul (basketball)](/wiki/Personal_foul_(basketball) "Personal foul (basketball)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/20130307_Cliff_Alexander_blocks_Paul_White_cropped.JPG/250px-20130307_Cliff_Alexander_blocks_Paul_White_cropped.JPG)](/wiki/File:20130307_Cliff_Alexander_blocks_Paul_White_cropped.JPG)

[Cliff Alexander](/wiki/Cliff_Alexander "Cliff Alexander") blocks a shot during the 2013 [IHSA](/wiki/Illinois_High_School_Association "Illinois High School Association") playoffs.

In [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), a **block** or **blocked shot** occurs when a defensive player legally deflects a [field goal attempt](/wiki/Field_goal_(basketball) "Field goal (basketball)") from an offensive player to prevent a score. The defender is not allowed to make contact with the offensive player's hand (unless the defender is also in contact with the ball) or a *[foul](/wiki/Foul_(basketball) "Foul (basketball)")* is called. In order to be legal, the block must occur while the shot is traveling upward or at its apex. A deflected field goal that is made does not count as a blocked shot and simply counts as a successful field goal attempt for shooter plus the points awarded to the shooting team. For the shooter, a blocked shot is counted as a missed field goal attempt. Also, on a shooting foul, a blocked shot cannot be awarded or counted, even if the player who deflected the field goal attempt is different from the player who committed the foul. If the ball is heading downward when the defender hits it, it is ruled as [goaltending](/wiki/Goaltending "Goaltending") and counts as a made basket. Goaltending is also called if the block is made after the ball bounces on the backboard ([NFHS](/wiki/National_Federation_of_State_High_School_Associations "National Federation of State High School Associations") excepted; the NCAA also used this rule until the 2009–10 season).

Nicknames for blocked shots include "rejections", "stuffs", "bushed", "spoinked", "fudged", or “double-fudged" (two-handed blocks), "facials", "swats", "denials", and "packs". Blocked shots were first officially recorded in the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") during the 1973–74 season.

Largely due to their height and position near the basket, [centers](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") and [power forwards](/wiki/Power_forward_(basketball) "Power forward (basketball)") tend to record the most blocks, but shorter players with good jumping ability can also be blockers, an example being [Dwyane Wade](/wiki/Dwyane_Wade "Dwyane Wade"), the shortest player, at 6'4", to record 100 blocked shots in a single season.[[1]](#cite_note-1) A player with the ability to block shots can be a positive asset to a team's defense, as they can make it difficult for opposing players to shoot near the basket and, if the blocked shot is kept in play rather than swatted [out of bounds](/wiki/Out_of_bounds "Out of bounds"), a blocked shot can lead to a fast break, a skill [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") was notable for.[[2]](#cite_note-Simmons2010-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) To be a good shot-blocker, a player needs great court sense and timing, and good height or jumping ability. One tactic is that a shot-blocker can intimidate opponents to alter their shots, resulting in a miss.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Chase-down block

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Block_(basketball)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Chase-down block")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Serbia_against_Slovenia_2.jpg/250px-Serbia_against_Slovenia_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Serbia_against_Slovenia_2.jpg)

[Mirza Begić](/wiki/Mirza_Begi%C4%87 "Mirza Begić") blocks [Duško Savanović](/wiki/Du%C5%A1ko_Savanovi%C4%87 "Duško Savanović") at the [EuroBasket 2011](/wiki/EuroBasket_2011 "EuroBasket 2011").

A chase-down block occurs when a player pursues an opposing player who had run ahead of the defense (as in a [fast break](/wiki/Fast_break "Fast break")), and then blocks their shot attempt. Often, the block involves hitting the ball into the backboard as the opponent tries to complete a [lay-up](/wiki/Lay-up "Lay-up"). One of the most recognized chase-down blocks is [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons")' [Tayshaun Prince](/wiki/Tayshaun_Prince "Tayshaun Prince")'s game-saving block on [Reggie Miller](/wiki/Reggie_Miller "Reggie Miller") in Game 2 of the [2004 NBA Eastern Conference Finals](/wiki/2004_NBA_Playoffs "2004 NBA Playoffs") against the [Indiana Pacers](/wiki/Indiana_Pacers "Indiana Pacers").[[4]](#cite_note-chase-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Abrams-5) Pistons announcer [Fred McLeod](/wiki/Fred_McLeod_(sportscaster) "Fred McLeod (sportscaster)"), who first witnessed this style of blocks from Prince, created the *chase-down* term later with the [Cleveland Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers").[[4]](#cite_note-chase-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Abrams-5) During the [2008–09 NBA season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_NBA_season "2008–09 NBA season"), the Cavaliers began tracking chase-down blocks, crediting [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James") with 23 that season and 20 the following season.[[4]](#cite_note-chase-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Abrams-5)[[6]](#cite_note-theplaindealer-6) Another landmark chase-down block occurred in the [2016 NBA Finals](/wiki/2016_NBA_Finals "2016 NBA Finals") when LeBron James, in the closing minutes of the 4th quarter delivered what became known as "[The Block](/wiki/The_Block_(basketball) "The Block (basketball)")" on a [lay-up](/wiki/Lay-up "Lay-up") attempt by [Andre Iguodala](/wiki/Andre_Iguodala "Andre Iguodala") with the score tied at 89 and 01:50 remaining in the game.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Shot-blocking records in the NBA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Block_(basketball)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Shot-blocking records in the NBA")]

* Most blocks in a single game: [Elmore Smith](/wiki/Elmore_Smith "Elmore Smith") (17)
* Most blocks in a single half: [Elmore Smith](/wiki/Elmore_Smith "Elmore Smith"), [George T. Johnson](/wiki/George_T._Johnson_(basketball) "George T. Johnson (basketball)"), [Manute Bol](/wiki/Manute_Bol "Manute Bol") (11 each)
* Most blocks per game in a season: [Mark Eaton](/wiki/Mark_Eaton "Mark Eaton") (5.56)
* Most career blocks: [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon") (3,830)
* Most blocks per game in a career: [Mark Eaton](/wiki/Mark_Eaton "Mark Eaton") (3.50)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* Most blocks in NBA Finals game: [Dwight Howard](/wiki/Dwight_Howard "Dwight Howard") (9)
* Most blocks in a non-NBA Finals playoff game: [Andrew Bynum](/wiki/Andrew_Bynum "Andrew Bynum"), [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon"), [Mark Eaton](/wiki/Mark_Eaton "Mark Eaton") (10 each)

## Shot-blocking records in NCAA Division I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Block_(basketball)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Shot-blocking records in NCAA Division I")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Brittney_Griner_3_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-Brittney_Griner_3_%28cropped2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brittney_Griner_3_(cropped2).jpg)

[Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner")

### Men

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Block_(basketball)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Men")]

* Most career blocks: [Jarvis Varnado](/wiki/Jarvis_Varnado "Jarvis Varnado") – [Mississippi State](/wiki/Mississippi_State_Bulldogs_basketball "Mississippi State Bulldogs basketball") (564) (2006–10)
* Most blocks single season, player: [David Robinson](/wiki/David_Robinson_(basketball) "David Robinson (basketball)") – [Navy](/wiki/Navy_Midshipmen_men%27s_basketball "Navy Midshipmen men's basketball") (207) (1985–86)
* Most blocks per game single season, player: [Shawn James](/wiki/Shawn_James_(basketball) "Shawn James (basketball)") – [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_Huskies_men%27s_basketball "Northeastern Huskies men's basketball") (6.53) (2005–06)
* Most blocks single season, team: [Kentucky](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball_team "2011–12 Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball team") (344) (2011–12)

### Women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Block_(basketball)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Women")]

* Most career blocks: [Brittney Griner](/wiki/Brittney_Griner "Brittney Griner") – [Baylor](/wiki/Baylor_Lady_Bears_basketball "Baylor Lady Bears basketball") (736)[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_(basketball)#endnote_a) (2009–13)
* Most blocks single season, player: Brittney Griner – Baylor (223) (2009–10)
* Most blocks per game single season, player: Brittney Griner – Baylor (6.4) (2009–10)
* Most blocks single season, team: [Baylor](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Baylor_Lady_Bears_basketball_team "2011–12 Baylor Lady Bears basketball team") (310) (2011–12)

And he's like, Listen, if you block a shot into the stands, the opposing team does nothing but get the ball back. And he said if you have the ability to block shots, why not keep it inbounds? He said don't swing at it. Direct it. I've never forgotten that.""
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-chase_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-chase_4-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-chase_4-2) The Plain Dealer, [Cleveland Cavaliers' LeBron James making the 'chase-down' block a signature move](http://www.cleveland.com/cavs/plaindealer/index.ssf?/base/sports/123745144820540.xml&coll=2) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20160509114843/http://www.cleveland.com/cavs/plaindealer/index.ssf?%2Fbase%2Fsports%2F123745144820540.xml&coll=2#) 2016-05-09 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"). Retrieved April 24, 2009.
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Abrams_5-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Abrams_5-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Abrams_5-2) Abrams, Jonathan (5 May 2010). ["On Defense, James Is Closer Than He Appears"](https://www.nytimes.com/2010/05/06/sports/basketball/06cavs.html?_r=0). *The New York Times*. Retrieved 11 June 2013.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-theplaindealer_6-0)** The Plain Dealer, [Cleveland Cavaliers star LeBron James finishes second in NBA Defensive Player of the Year voting](http://www.cleveland.com/cavs/plaindealer/index.ssf?/base/sports/1240389114135630.xml&coll=2) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20160414114132/http://www.cleveland.com/cavs/plaindealer/index.ssf?%2Fbase%2Fsports%2F1240389114135630.xml&coll=2#) 2016-04-14 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), accessed April 22, 2009.

8. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** Greenberg, Chris (June 20, 2016). ["LeBron James gave Cleveland an iconic sports moment it wants to remember: 'The Block'"](https://www.boston.com/sports/sports-news/2016/06/20/lebron-james-block). *[Boston.com](/wiki/Boston.com "Boston.com")*. Boston Globe Media Partners, LLC. Retrieved July 3, 2016. "Cleveland, no matter how hard it may have tried, couldn't forget 'The Drive' or 'The Fumble' or 'The Shot.' But now, thanks to LeBron James, it has a sports moment requiring the definite article that it will want to remember forever: The Block."
